captured.evidence: The central compartment includes the brain and other VRG organs
Context-sensitive half-time means the drug half-life changes depending on how long you give it

missed.description: Single-bolus awakening is due to redistribution from brain to peripheral tissues, not hepatic metabolism
After prolonged infusion, peripheral compartments saturate, eliminating redistribution gradient
First-pass pulmonary uptake buffers peak arterial concentration
Elderly patients have smaller central compartments requiring dose reduction

errors.statement: The bolus patient wakes up fast because propofol is rapidly metabolized by the liver
errors.correction: Rapid awakening after bolus is due to redistribution from brain to peripheral tissues, not hepatic metabolism

errors.statement: after 4 hours the liver enzymes are overwhelmed and cannot keep up
errors.correction: Hepatic metabolism rate remains constant; delayed emergence is due to peripheral compartment saturation eliminating redistribution

errors.statement: Elderly patients might need higher doses because they have more body fat
errors.correction: Elderly patients need lower doses due to smaller central compartments causing higher peak concentrations

summary: You correctly identified the central compartment components and context-sensitive half-time concept, but fundamentally misunderstood that rapid awakening is due to redistribution, not metabolism. Your explanation of prolonged infusion effects and elderly dosing requirements are backwards.
voss_quip: Redistribution, not metabolism. The liver doesn't get tired after lunch.
